ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated macaroni-and-cheese entry; A0CKW (pasta dish) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for macaroni and cheese as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of mac and cheese is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one medium bowl as commonly served in a cafeteria or casual dining setting.

nutrient_source.title: Mac and Cheese — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F05060076)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05060076 for Mac and Cheese. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Macaroni and cheese, homemade' (FDC ID 172131) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 48 is based on macaroni (white, boiled) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Mac and cheese as a composite dish has a slightly lower effective GI than plain boiled macaroni due to the fat and protein from the cheese sauce dampening glycaemic response; GI 48 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 61.1) / 100 = 29.3 for a 250 g serving (24.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5 = 61.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 379 mg/100 g with limited potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less, use low-sodium cheese, reduce added salt, and add potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to improve the sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined macaroni base with 24.4 g carbohydrate/100 g and low dietary fibre (1.56 g/100 g) producing a moderate glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 150 g, substitute wholemeal or legume-based pasta to increase fibre and reduce the glycaemic response, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese sauce (~1.5 g/100 g) combined with elevated sodium (379 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should reduce portion size, choose reduced-fat cheese, replace butter with unsaturated oil in the sauce, and increase vegetable content to lower saturated fat and sodium density per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate and dairy saturated fat with no meaningful anti-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should use wholemeal pasta, reduce cheese quantity, add anti-inflammatory vegetables such as broccoli or spinach, and replace butter with extra-virgin olive oil in the sauce preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mac and cheese (macaroni and cheese) is a classic Western comfort food dish consisting of cooked elbow macaroni pasta enveloped in a creamy, savoury cheese sauce typically made from cheddar cheese, milk, butter, and a flour-based roux. The dish has a soft, tender pasta texture with a rich, smooth, and mildly tangy sauce. It is widely consumed across the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia, and is popular in Singapore and Malaysia as a casual Western meal option. Per 100 g, it provides 140 kcal, 4.31 g protein, 2.75 g fat, 24.44 g carbohydrate, 2.85 g sugar, 1.56 g dietary fibre, and 379 mg sodium. The refined macaroni base produces a moderate glycaemic load, and the sodium content from cheese and added salt places it in the borderline range for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian and contains gluten and dairy allergens. It is not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diets. Clinically, it is best consumed in controlled portions by individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, and can be improved nutritionally by substituting wholemeal pasta, reducing cheese quantity, and adding non-starchy vegetables.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05060076 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest match (homemade macaroni and cheese, FDC ID 172131) and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for white macaroni (boiled) as the closest approved analogous food.
notes: Saturated fat (1.50 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 172131 (Macaroni and cheese, homemade) as HPB Foodo F05060076 does not report saturated fat breakdown. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in HPB Foodo F05060076 and detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific Singapore-context preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or other approved sources as of 2026-04-16. The dietary_tags field in the input listed 'low_calorie'; at 140 kcal/100 g this is moderate-calorie and the tag has not been carried forward as it is not clinically defensible without a defined reference standard. Region tag 'malaysia' from input retained in cultural context but region_of_origin set to United States / United Kingdom as the dish's canonical origin. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on white macaroni (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI may be slightly lower due to fat and protein from cheese sauce.
